This song was first played at the Dave & Tim show on 7.28.07. The meaning of the name was revealed when Stefan said in an interview on Sirius satellite radio that this is the twenty-seventh song from the latest batch of songs the band has been working on. It's unclear when exactly Dave started numbering his songs again, but it was certainly no earlier than the Stand Up sessions.The riff for #27 may have come from the intro to Hello Again, much like Angel's riff came from an intro to Jimi Thing.On the 2010 Europe tour, the official setlist page began listing the song as "#27 (Arms of a Women)" [sic] after Dave introduced the song by the subtitle.A regular feature on the 2007 tour, #27 has seen a slow decline in number of plays each subsequent year since then.

songs.originalArtist: haines fullerton, an early collaborator who took his own life in september 1996
songs.songHistory: This song made a brief span of appearances in early 1993. It was inspired by and written for Miguel Valdez, a percussionist who sat in with the band in 1992 and who passed away in early 1993. It was finally released in 1994 on Under the Table and Dreaming (track #34) as an instrumental track. The song is co-written by Haines Fullerton, an early collaborator who took his own life in September 1996. As of 2014, this is the only song to drop out of rotation before its official studio release: by the time Under the Table came out, #34 hadn't been played live in a year and a half and wouldn't be played again for almost eleven more years.The song made a surprising comeback on 7.9.05 after a few teases earlier in the tour. This is currently the longest comeback in DMB history, at 4491 days between performances, or a little over 12 years.  It was played again with some lyrics (for the first time since 1993) on 10.2.07 to celebrate Dave's wife's 34th birthday; it then disappeared again until returning in 2013 for a tease, two partials, and two full performances (one of which was on LeRoi's birthday). It was again played on LeRoi's birthday in 2018.Because this has become an instrumental song, it is difficult to objectively distinguish between teases and partial performances. We consider a performance partial if it has the main riff at least four times (constituting a "verse") followed by the "we'll be okay" part twice (constituting a "chorus").The song was the subject of a clue on the July 12, 2012 episode of "Jeopardy!"Category: 30s RockClue: Track 34 on this band's "Under the Table and Dreaming" is cleverly titled #34.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - acoustic guitar

songs.albumName: Big Whiskey and the GrooGrux King
songs.songHistory: This short hidden track at the end of Big Whiskey and the Groogrux King features Dave and Carter playing behind a repeating saxophone riff played by LeRoi Moore.Much like #27 isn't the twenty-seventh song the band wrote, this is not the thirty-fifth song the band ever wrote; however, the exact meaning behind the title has not yet been explained.  The song #34 is a tribute to fallen comrade Miguel Valdes and placed at the end of the album.  It is possible this is titled in a way that it signifies the next tribute, this one to Leroi Moore.It is not listed on the back of the album and is not given its own individual track, but the title has been registered and was listed on early promotional materials for the album. The Spotify and YouTube versions of the album, however, track the song separately from You & Me and give it the title #35.

#36 started out as a jammy song in 1993. It was inspired as a result of the tragic assassination of a South African freedom fighter, Chris Hani, and was played for the first time three days after his death in 1993. After serving as a tribute song for almost all of 1994, the song eventually evolved into an inspiration jam about freedom featuring scatting by Dave and a monumental drum solo intro by Carter. Dave noted on 10.9.94 that he changed the lyrics from "Hani, Hani" to "honey, honey," to fit more with the song's mood (though the familiar "come and dance with me" chorus had taken shape by late 1993).1995 through 1996 marked the golden years of this song, where it enjoyed frequent highlights and dancing on the part of the fans.  It was recorded in the Crash sessions, but didn't make the final cut (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998). In 1999 and 2000, the song returned in a new, slower form featuring the Lovely Ladies and without the Carter solo.During the Everyday studio sessions, the song underwent a huge transformation to become the song Everyday. Since then, the spirit of #36 has resurfaced within Everyday in the following forms: 1) Dave sings "Honey Honey" in the intro/outro. 2) Boyd plays the #36 "Honey Honey" melody in the intro/outro3) The song ends in the same fashion as #36 did4) Everyday directly segues into a #36 reprise consisting of a verse and chorus from #36 (e.g. 5.26.01).Here is the original Reuters announcement of Hani's death:April 10, 1993, Saturday, BC cycle AFRICAN NATIONAL CONGRESS OFFICIAL IS ASSASSINATED JOHANNESBURG, SOUTH AFRICA   A senior official of the African National Congress was assassinated on Saturday, the South African Press Association reported.      Chris Hani, 50, was also the leader of the South African Communist Party.    SAPA quoted police as saying Hani was shot at his home in Boksburg, a largely white suburb east of Johannesburg.    It quoted a police spokesman as saying a man had been arrested.    A neighbor of Hani said the

This song debuted in early 1995 as "40 Always," a calm and mellow love song about devotion. After a string of performances in the Spring Tour of 1995, the song disappeared altogether for 3 years. In the summer of 1998, fans began bringing signs of #40 to shows and handing Dave tapes of the definitive Yoshi's performance. That fall, the song started being teased; finally to resurface in 1999 as a Dave solo feature.The song has made a lot of appearances since summer 1999 to various extents.  We require a partial version to at least go through the part of the song that usually has the word "always" (though occasionally other lyrics are sung during this part).  A tease is anything short of that mark, and a full version is at least two verses and two choruses.

This song was originally known as "41 Police," a name derived from a combination of its being the forty-first song Dave wrote and its sounding like a Police song. "Police" was most likely not intended to be a final name for the song but rather a way of identifying it from other songs when it was new (see also "40 Always").After being perfected during the studio recording sessions of Crash, #41 came to be the most played song of 1996 and an instant classic. When Béla Fleck and the Flecktones guested on the song on NYE 1996, a marriage was made between #41 and the Flecktones song "Sojourn of Arjuna". Since then #41-Sojourn has become a tradition between DMB and the Flecktones whenever they play together. The Flecktones have even played a bit of #41 during their own performances of Sojourn of Arjuna.#41 has been played every year since its debut and has been a regular feature of both Dave Matthews Band and Dave & Tim setlists, with the exception of 2006, when it was a relative rarity.Interpolations:Caprice No. 24 - Niccolò Paganini"Common Free Style" - The RH Factor (interpolated in nearly every performance since June 2014)"Everyday" - Dave Matthews Band"Fantasy" - Earth, Wind & Fire"Getaway" - Earth, Wind & Fire"Girl from Ipanema" - Astrud Gilberto"If You Want Me to Stay" - Sly & the Family Stone"Jeannie" (theme from I Dream of Jeannie)"My Favorite Things" (from The Sound of Music)"Red Rain" - Peter Gabriel"Sojourn of Arjuna" - Béla Fleck & the Flecktones"Spain" - Chick Corea"Take Me Out to the Ball Game""What You Won't Do for Love" - Bobby Caldwell"When You Wish Upon a Star" (from Pinnochio)"You Can't Rollerskate in a Buffalo Herd" - Roger MillerTheme from New York, New York - Liza Minnelli/Frank SinatraTheme from The Six Million Dollar ManStudio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

songs.title: (Don't Fear) The Reaper
songs.songHistory: This song originally appears on Blue Öyster Cult's 1976 album Agents of Fortune. While it was a hit on its own in the 1970s, it is perhaps most well known today from its featuring in a 2000 Saturday Night Live skit, which lampoons the song's iconic use of a cowbell.This cover was played by Yukon Kornelius at their first show, complete with Jason Biggs playing cowbell, and it has become a staple of Yukon Kornelius's (few) shows.Among several cover songs that DMB debuted on the 2011 Caravan tour, this one was played only at The Gorge, on 9.3.11. It seemed to have disappeared afterward, but it, along with fellow Caravan cover Good Times Bad Times, made a reappearance in 2013. Before the 6.28.13 performance, Dave mentioned that LeRoi had always wanted the band to cover the song. On 7.2.13, Rashawn played cowbell on the song—none of the previous DMB versions featured cowbell.Both bands have played only the single edit of the song, which omits a lengthy guitar solo.

It has been covered by many artists, the most recognizable version being recorded by Elvis Costello and the Attractions.

songs.title: Again and Again
songs.songHistory: This song (originally known as "Bob Law"), along with Bismarck and Here On Out, was one of three songs whose titles were leaked from the band's studio sessions in 2015. When played on 5.7.16, Dave said that it didn't have a name yet; however, this title was printed on the setlist. After a few plays, Dave began calling the song "blah blah blah," which the "Bob Law" title seems to be a play on (and also a possible Arrested Development reference). When the tracklist for Come Tomorrow was revealed, Mark Batson confirmed on Instagram that the song had been retitled to "Again and Again."

songs.title: Alcañiz (Festiva)
songs.songHistory: This song is often labeled as "Segovia," as Tim has said that he doesn't remember exactly which song it is; he has said that it comes from Spanish guitarist Andrés Segovia's "Castillos de España," and a thorough listening to that work leaves "Alcañiz (Festiva)" as the only possible piece. Tim's arrangement of the song sounds significantly reworked from the original, however, so there is always a possibility that this is still not the correct name.

songs.title: All Along the Watchtower
songs.songHistory: "Watchtower" is one of the all time most played encore songs. The band is thought to have been covering this song since at least their third show ever. Dave was known to have been covering it solo since before the band came together. The song became an early favorite; at many older shows, the crowd would scream "Watchtower" throughout the entire show. Watchtower is one of the most guested songs and features many popular jams. It sounds hardly anything like Bob Dylan's original nor the more well-known Jimi Hendrix cover. The song even has evolved over the years, dropping a verse, gaining a Stefan solo feature intro, and a high note during "the wind began to howl." Due to the chord progression's similarity to the ending section of Led Zeppelin's "Stairway to Heaven," DMB's performances of this song have occasionally featured lyrical and/or musical references to Stairway, most notably on 12.1.98 and on the vast majority of performances from 2008–2009 and 2018–present."All Along the Watchtower" originally appears on Bob Dylan's 1967 album John Wesley Harding. Jimi Hendrix's cover appears on the Jimi Hendrix Experience's 1968 album Electric Ladyland, as well as on just about every Hendrix compilation.

Lyrically, although there are few lyrics, a general theme can be derived through the following evidence: 1) War theme: Album version features gunfire/war sounds 2) Matrimony/Lovers theme: "She said... yeah"3) Soldier going off to war: 5.27.07 Alternate Lyrics4) Unwilling distance separation of lovers: 6.7.05 Improv Jam lyrics, 7.27.05 Two Step Intro lyrics.It was played most extensively in the 2005 Summer Tour. The 2006 Summer Tour saw the song transition into being played almost exclusively in the encore 1 slot. The song then went missing in 2008 through the Summer of 2010 where it made a one-time return after 251 shows and 1016 days and also seeing a couple of plays during the 2011 caravan shows.The longest version to date was played during the 4th night of the historic 2005 Red Rocks run on 9.12.2005 with only a handful of future versions ever coming close on time. songs.title: An' Another Thing
songs.songHistory: This is essentially the same song as Little Thing. The name seems to have been changed to "An' Another Thing" at the last minute before the release of Some Devil, as it was called "Little Thing" in the initial press release, and a picture in the album's booklet shows that it was referred to as such in the studio, as well. When Dave and Tim played the song on 1.14.04, it was once again called "Little Thing," and all subsequent setlists for which we have scans list it as such. We have kept this song's page here for completion's sake, but we've decided to continue calling post-Some Devil performances "Little Thing."

songs.title: Ants Marching
songs.songHistory: As Dave Matthews once put it, "This song is our anthem". It's perhaps one of the best known DMB songs and the all time most played set closer in history by a longshot. It incorporates every aspect of the band's personalities and bridges the DMB of old with the new. During its first years, Ants Marching was an instant favorite. It was usually preceeded by the 2001 Intro and was heavy on the keyboards. After Peter Greisar left the band, the intro evolved into a prelude build up similar to "Dueling Banjos". As the song progressed through 1993, it evolved its most recognizable feature: the Carter snare intro, which has sometimes lasted as long as a minute and a half. 1995 was the golden year of Ants. Not only was it the most played set closer, but it was also the most played song. It was during this time that Dave began letting the crowd sing "People in every direction". Further through the years, the song evolved slightly more to include a 1-2-3 round robin jam towards the end and has also featured guests on special occasions. According to calculations based on the number of times played and number of shows performed, any given show has about a 50% chance of containing Ants. On our site, snare intro in the notes denotes the Carter snare intro, direct intro denotes the quick the band intro.Starting in 2007, Rashawn began interpolating The Jacksons' "Shake Your Body (Down to the Ground)" after the second chorus in nearly every version of the song.

songs.title: Any Noise
songs.songHistory: This predecessor of Say Goodbye began appearing as periodic Dave solo features during the summer and early fall of 1993, when Dave used to frequently begin each show with several solo performances. During its early days the song's name was disputed by varying perceptions of whether Dave was singing "any noise" or "anti-noise," so it became common to simply write both as the title. According to our lyrical transcribers, he is clearly singing "any noise," but the moniker has stuck. It's worth noting that while it's become standard to put both spellings in writing, it's only necessary to say one of them, as the discrepancy comes from the fact that they are pronounced the same way.It could be that Dave always knew the song as "Say Goodbye" given the fact that he introduced it as such on 9.1.93. However, some elements of the song remained separate from Say Goodbye and actually made a brief appearance in 1995 as a partial song during one of Dave's solo encore performances.

songs.title: Anyone Seen the Bridge
songs.songHistory: This famous segue originated from a jam that was performed after So Much to Say on 10.3.96 with Soul Coughing's M. Doughty. The song had a resemblence to Led Zeppelin's "The Crunge" where Robert Plant says, "Has anyone seen the bridge?"  Somehow, the title stuck. Two days later the band decided to play it as a bridge in between So Much To Say and Too Much. The jam was an instant classic. After making a cameo in Before These Crowded Streets, the song went on to become one of the main highlights featuring the band's energy in 1998. In 2000, the song was involved in the suprising So Much to Say > ASTB > Too Much (fake) > Ants Marching sequence.  In the summer of 2003, ASTB turned into a segue for So Much To Say to go to a number of songs including Rapunzel, Spoon, and Warehouse; however, it has since reverted to nearly always segueing into Too Much, or at least a Too Much fake.Around 2007–08, the band used a "freeze time" transition between So Much to Say and Anyone Seen the Bridge, where they would all freeze in place for a few seconds while Carter counted out a number/tempo through the band's IEM system. They would then play that number of "hits" of the last note of So Much to Say before freezing again and repeating a few times, sometimes pausing for quite a while between rounds. The "freeze time" transition got most creative when, on 7.29.08 and 9.6.08, the band played the "hits" to the rhythm of the Tripping Billies intro. [Note: Although this intro comes before Dave sings the final "little baby" from So Much to Say, we consider it part of Anyone Seen the Bridge in order to be consistent with times when the band played it without having played So Much to Say at all.]

songs.title: Back to Alexandra
songs.songHistory: This is song by Big Voice Jack, a legendary South African pennywhistle player which he wrote to give vent to his lifelong hatred for guns. How he came into contact with DMB is an interesting story. He was playing at a club in South Africa when Dave was in the audience. Roi had asked Dave to pick up a few pennywhistles while he was visiting South Africa and Dave approached Big Voice Jack after the gig to ask him where he could find them. Big Voice Jack decided to rather give his own whistles to Dave. "I thought that I would never play in a big stadium in America, so I wanted my whistles to be there," says Jack. "So I gave them to him." Jack finally did get to play in a stadium when he guested with DMB on this song on 6.7.98.In the summer of 1998, the band repaid his generosity by inviting him to come to the States and play with them on the big stadium stages.In the Back to Alexandra documentary, Dave mentions that the band had heard this song on a CD that Lerole had made of his band's songs, but that CD doesn't appear to have ever been officially released.Alexandra is a town in South Africa near Johannesburg and is Lerole's hometown.

songs.title: Bartender
songs.songHistory: This song of epic proportions originally came into the light as "Reconcile our Differences" / "On Bended Knee" on the 1999 Dave and Tim winter tour. Many claim the song sounded eerily like Leave Me Praying and possibly co-evolved from the same song as Don't Drink the Water. Reconcile Our Differences went through several stages of lyrical evolutions after finally being realized as Bartender by the end of the tour. However, it wasn't until the Summer of 2000 that the song finally came into its own after being reworked in the Lillywhite Studio Sessions into a climactic masterpiece.  Times for this song are far from an exact science.  When it is played out of Big Eyed Fish, song time starts as soon as Boyd's droning intro becomes the most prominent thing you hear.

songs.title: Beach Ball
songs.songHistory: Beach Ball was brand new song that was recorded during the Big Whiskey sessions and released on the Little Red Bird bonus disc included with the deluxe edition boxed set.  Nothing was known about the song at all until its debut 4.29.09 mid-set night 2 in Alpharetta, Georgia.  After that performance, a brief interpolation by Jeff and Rashawn was noticed in the 4.15.09 version of Two Step.  It is played on the raised B guitar that is also used for You Never Know, Stay or Leave, Sister, etc.  The version played that night was the same structure and length as the studio version but when it was played again at the next show two days later on 5.1.09 at The Woodlands, it contained an extended section in the middle that included a small, controlled Boyd solo and a duet between Rashawn and Jeff before coming back into the final pre-chorus and chorus.  The version was two and a half minutes longer than the debut and was released as an online-only bonus track on August 11, 2009 to fans who ordered the Big Whiskey and the GrooGrux King: iTunes Pass.  Despite the heavy play of Big Whiskey songs over the next three years, Beach Ball wasn't played again though it was one of the more well-received songs from the sessions.  Signs at a show in Tampa on 7.28.10 prompted Dave to play an impromptu first verse and chorus, accompanied by some light percussion from Carter before it gave way in a segue to Stay or Leave.  Finally, it was played for the first time Dave & Tim on 12.10.10 in Las Vegas.  This version didn't contain the middle jam, though Tim solos replaced the horn lead between verses.  However, the soft instrumental outro from the album was repeated a handful of extra times while Tim soloed over it.  Before this version, Dave said that he liked the song, but didn't play it often and asked for luck.  Afterwards, he said his puppy Angus would destroy his beach ball.

songs.title: Bed's Too Big Without You, The

songs.title: Best of What's Around, The
songs.songHistory: "BOWA" is one of those songs that has been around since the beginning, yet hasn't changed much throughout the years. In the early years, it was often played as a Dave solo feature early on in the setlist. As the years passed, its role remained strong as a great song to play early in the setlist to set the dmb mood for a show. It also briefly served as a great 1-2 combo after the old Two Steps of 94-95. In 2000 and 2001, the song served as a feature for the Lovely Ladies, who added their voices to the spirit of the song. Surprisingly, the Spring 2002 tour was the first tour in which BOWA was a missing song. It has become a relative rarity since 2000, but it still has yet to go an entire year without being played at least once.

songs.title: Big Eyed Fish
songs.songHistory: Also known as "One Eyed Fish," this is one of the few songs to have been teased in concert before it was fully played. Having been recorded in the Lillywhite Studio Sessions, Big Eyed Fish was released through a demo leak and became incredibly popular with fans. Known to have been soundchecked in 2000, fans knew the song had a high potential to be played in 2001 and drew up signs for it early on on the 2001 summer tour. And as the teases foreshadowed, the song made its debut in the encore of 5.14.01 as "Fish Monkey Man" on the 2001 Summer Tour. It quickly developed into the band's showpiece as classic 1-2 combo with Bartender featuring Roi on the Pennywhistle bridge. Since then, it has only been played full band a few times without preceding Bartender. When it is played into Bartender, song time ends as soon as Boyd's droning intro becomes the most prominent thing you hear.

It is credited to Lennon/McCartney, like most of the Beatles' songs, but was written by Paul McCartney.

songs.title: Blue Water
songs.songHistory: Blue Water (also known as "Blue Water Baboon Farm") made its greatest contribution early on in 92-93. The song featured a 180 degree mood change to live shows and displayed a more serious and experimental band to the audience. It formed a frequent 1-2 combo with Minarets, but was eventually replaced in the combo by Typical Situation. After the fall of 1993 the song all but disappeared from sets, making brief comeback appearances on 2.25.95 and on the Dave and Tim tours of 1996 and 1997. After having been assumed dead, the song made a surprising comeback on the Fall 2000 Tour as a Dave solo feature. It made an even bigger comeback on the Spring 2009 Tour as a full-band feature, with more and more of the song being played each night before suddenly disappearing again.In July 2007, Stefan posted a thread on izstyle.com asking what song DMB fans missed the most.  The overwhelming winner was Blue Water. He posted: "I loved the responses and am with you on Blue Water.... One of my all time favorites. The solo section bass line from that song was inspired from listening to Pink Floyd all the time when I was 18. The lyrics...beautifully dark...we were a young band when that song was written......Look what you all have done to me, I'm feeling a bit melancholy at the moment :-)Till Tour"For the purposes of distinguishing between full and partial versions of this song, we define a full version as one that contains the lyrics and a jam/solo section. Partial versions have typically omitted the jam/solo section. Teases generally end before the "take this my arms" verse. [NOTE: Previously, we defined a full version as having a return to the lyrics at the end; however, we discovered that not all of the song's original performances had this part, so we revised our criteria.]This song has been officially released twice with printed copyright information (Warehouse 8 Vol. 1 and Live Trax 20), and both instances give it a copyright date of 1990, which seems inaccurate.

songs.title: Break for It

songs.title: Break Free
songs.composer: Beauford / Lessard / Matthews / Moore / Tinsley / Batson
songs.songHistory: Break Free was one of a handful of songs that sprung from early 2006 studio sessions with Mark Batson.  It debuted on 6.13.06 in Toronto during a stretch of shows that also saw debuts of The Idea of You, Kill the King and Shotgun. Its lyrics are in the same vein as Hunger For the Great Light, with thinly-veiled erotic themes sung over a sleazy baritone guitar riff and Boyd's electric mandolin.The original structure of the song contained a main portion that included verses and choruses followed by an extended loose jam featuring a reserved sax solo by LeRoi, then a trumpet solo by Rashawn that increased the intensity to a climactic ending. It was played this way only three times.  On 6.21.06, it was played with a tightened structure that eliminated the final verse and LeRoi's solo.  This edit reduced the song by a minute or so and accelerated the slow, patient build found in earlier versions.  It stayed strong throughout the 2006 Summer Tour, mostly rotating nights with Idea of You.  The two songs were played a combined 47 times on the tour after Break Free's debut, but only at the same show 5 times.After the 2006 Summer Tour, it hadn't been played or mentioned again until it was played on the second night of the 2011 Atlantic City Caravan 6.25.11.  The song was played three times in four+ stops giving hope that it would make a full come back.  While there was no studio version officially released until 2023, a studio recording captured via IEM played at a soundcheck through the soundboard made its way around long before then. It is obviously a rough cut, with hardly final editing done. It contains backing vocals and overdubs, but no jam after the proper song concludes.The song has two live releases, the first on Live Trax 6 from 7.8.06 and a version featuring future traveling band member Jeff Coffin on sax from 8.15.06 that was released on the Warehouse 5 vol. 8 fan club disc.  There is no official release of a version with the original structure yet.

The lyrics are rough in spots, but there's no way to tell whether the take is an early take or whether the song remained unfinished after it.

It became one of their more popular songs, reaching #9 on the US Charts in the year of its release. Chris Frantz thought of the titular chorus after seeing a Parliament-Funkadelic show where the crowd chanted "Burn down the house."

songs.title: Busted Stuff
songs.songHistory: This jazzy number was the last Lillywhite Sessions song to debut on the Summer 2000 Tour featuring Butch Taylor and the Lovely Ladies. Dave introduced it as "She My Bitch" at its first few performances (this was likely never a serious title but rather an early, improvised studio lyric from the "she my one" bridge).The song turned up missing in 2001 but made a return in early 2002. About two months before the Busted Stuff album was released, however, it again went missing for about eight years aside from two Dave & Tim performances in 2003, and it hadn't been played since 2010 until it once again returned in 2023.

songs.title: Butterfly
songs.songHistory: First appearing on the Dave and Friends 2003 Winter Tour, Butterfly is a lullaby-like song Dave wrote while filming for “Because of Winn Dixie” and says he, “was thinking about my little girls”(01.16.2004). It was played sparingly until achieving moderate success towards the end of the 2006 Summer Tour as a Dave solo encore opener. Dave encourages listeners to live life despite the sudden and often harsh bumps in the road. Although it does not appear on the film’s soundtrack album, a recording of the song was released on a CD sent to Academy voters for Oscar consideration for Best Original song.

songs.title: Captain
songs.songHistory: On 6.3.96 at an Agents of Good Roots show, a heavily inebriated Dave was asked to come on stage and play a few tunes on Andrew Winn's guitar. One of those was this song, which fans eventually labeled "Crazy," based on the opening lyric. Crazy debuted as a feature seven months later on the Dave and Tim '97 tour, where it underwent some slight lyrical evolution and seemed to have been fully developed; however, the song was missing from the Before These Crowded Streets album and only appeared once on the Dave and Tim '99 tour, where Dave admitted to having forgotten the title. In years following that tour, it seemed as though the song had been forgotten and would never get a chance at full-band play.That all changed in the recording studio of the 2000 Steve Lillywhite sessions, where the song was arranged for the full band and formally titled "Captain." The song was left out of the 2000 tour, however. After the Lillywhite Sessions leaked in March 2001, the song finally surfaced in the following Summer 2001 tour, first as a quick tease, then as a longer, partial version. It was finally released on Busted Stuff in 2002 with reworked lyrics, and it was played live by the full band for the first time on the day of the album's release.When the band began playing the song in 2002, they added a second bridge and chorus section, which we label the "extended version." Performances without this note have the same arrangement as the album, even if they are longer performances due to a guest.Until 2011, the song had only been played by the band twice since 2002 and not at all since 2004. It has been a rarity since then, finding its way into the rotation at least once every few years.

songs.title: Cha Cha
songs.songHistory: This song was registered for copyright under the title "Cha Cha" on October 19, 2016. It was first played live on 6.16.23 and was written as "Cha Cha (Only Takes a Moment)" on the handwritten setlist, "Cha Cha" on the printed setlist, and "Only Takes a Moment" on the official band website's setlist. Dave introduced the song as essentially untitled for the first few performances but on 7.11.23 introduced it as "Cha Cha." If/when a version appears on a live release or on the band's YouTube channel, we will have a better idea of its proper title and will adjust accordingly.

songs.title: Christmas Song
songs.songHistory: This quiet tune started out as a Dave Solo feature in early shows, and was included in Remember Two Things as one of the Dave and Tim tracks on the album compilation. Throughout the years, this song has regularly been played during the the pre-holiday season, paying ode to the story of a man who had a profound impact on humanity. This song has always featured Carter on backing vocals and was recorded during the Everyday studio recording sessions for release on the Platinum Christmas compilation disc. During live play, Dave usually quotes a line from The Beatles' "All You Need is Love" and "Can't Buy Me Love".When introducing the song, Dave often gives a disclaimer that the song is "not a religious song, just a story" (09.18.10).The song's debut (introduced that night as "Blood of Our Children") was long thought to be 12.23.92.  When the unknown 12.21.92 show was released by management on June 8, 2010 as part of the DMBlive series, Christmas Song appeared in the encore, moving its known debut date back two days.  There is no dialogue before or after the song to mark its first time being played, so it is still possible that the real debut came earlier than this even.

songs.title: Christmas Time Is Here
songs.songHistory: This highlight was played on 12.7.98 thanks to the jazz-loving Butch Taylor.

songs.title: Cigarette Lit
songs.songHistory: This song was recorded during the studio session for Some Devil. In a Guitar World interview, Dave is quoted as saying, "With Some Devil, there was one song we axed called "Cigarette Lit," which despite being a great song didn't end up on the album because it was slightly oversized; its chest was a little too full compared to everything else. When I rejected it, I asked myself, 'Am I being a moron?' Now, I'm happy with that decision. Sometimes you have to let things be what they are."The song contains chord progressions from Hold Me Down, which enjoyed a few live appearances in 1995 and 1996.

Johnson - drums

songs.title: Colonel Bogey March
songs.songHistory: This military march is probably best known from its appearance in the film The Bridge on the River Kwai, in which British prisoners of war whistle the song while marching. The tune is often paired with the following lyrics, in which case it is known as "Hitler Has Only Got One Ball":Hitler has only got one ballGoering has two, but very smallHimmler has something similarBut poor old Goebbels has no balls at allThese lyrics were originally to have been sung in Kwai, but they were deemed too vulgar at the time and were therefore replaced by the well-known whistling.

songs.title: Come and Go
songs.songHistory: This song appeared on Down Boy Down's self-titled album.

songs.title: Come Away
songs.songHistory: Dave wrote the music for this song, from Shakespeare's Twelfth Night (II.iv.58-73), for a production of the play at his daughters' school in June 2013. For the live production, Dave played the piano while the student playing the character Feste sang the lyrics. He did the same for another song from the play, Oh Mistress Mine.Dave posted an at-home recording to his Tumblr in 2013 (it has since been removed but is still available mirrored on YouTube).Dave interpolated lyrics from the song into the What You Are prelude on 5.17.13.

songs.title: Come Together
songs.songHistory: Dave recited a few lines from this song on 9.30.92 in a spoken word manner.Tim played it in his solo spot on 10.30.06.Dave guested on this song with Blues Traveler on 7.20.96 in Austin, TX.

songs.title: Come Tomorrow
songs.songHistory: Brandi Carlile provides backing vocals on the album version of this song. A remix, with Carlile's vocals higher in the mix, was released as a single and credited to Dave Matthews Band feat. Brandi Carlile.

songs.title: Communication
songs.songHistory: Dave guests on this song on Béla Fleck and the Flecktones' 1998 album Left of Cool.He guested on it at just about every show the Flecktones opened on the Fall 1998 DMB tour.

songs.title: Continue

songs.songHistory: This Neil Young original came into the limelight at the Bridge School Benefit of 2000 with Neil Young guesting. Warren Haynes, whose band Gov't Mule also covers this song, has also contributed his vocals and guitar to in some monumental versions of this song.

songs.title: Counting the Stars
songs.songHistory: This song from the Everyday sessions has been registered with ASCAP. It may have evolved into You Never Know.

songs.title: Court of the Crimson King, The
songs.songHistory: This King Crimson song is from their 1969 album In the Court of the Crimson King. It has only been played as a Tim solo at DMB-related shows, the first in Mexico on 1.26.25.

songs.title: Cowgirl in the Sand
songs.songHistory: This Neil Young cover appears on Down Boy Down's self-titled album.

songs.title: Crash Into Me
songs.songHistory: Crash Into Me was written during the Crash recording sessions in '95. It briefly peeped its head out during a Dave and Tim live soundcheck during a break from the studio. Along with Too Much, this song was played live before the album release date in April of 96. After the album release the song became immensely popular with fans and has enjoyed frequent playing since. Starting on the 2001 Summer Tour the song surprisingly became a rarity, but it has since returned to regular rotation. The "Dixie Chicken" interpolation is also very popular with fans, and is included in the song 90% of the time.In the 2018 "Producers Roundtable" promotional video, John Alagia mentions that an earlier version of this song was titled "Soldier."

songs.title: Crazy
songs.songHistory: Dave has said that the beginning of Crush was lifted directly from the beginning of this Willie Nelson song.Early versions of Captain were originally named "Crazy" by fans but are unrelated to this song.

songs.title: Crazy Easy
songs.songHistory: Crazy Easy (sometimes written with a hyphen between the two words) was one of five new songs on the 2004 Summer tour. It was played at nearly every show on that tour and featured a different set of improvised lyrics each night. It was also the first of the 2004 songs to disappear from setlists after not being played at all on the 2004 Vote for Change tour. By the time it was finally played again in 2013, Dave had written lyrics for it and changed his guitar part slightly.

songs.title: Crossing, The
songs.songHistory: In 2018, Dave Matthews joined a group of about 50 South African musicians for a studio recording of this Johnny Clegg song under the moniker "Friends of Johnny Clegg."

songs.title: Crossroads
songs.songHistory: Boyd guested on this song with Brooks Young Band on 3.1.13.

songs.title: Crush
songs.songHistory: This wildly popular love song from "Before These Crowded Streets" was part of the new onslaught of songs recorded during those studio sessions and quickly evolved an extended jam in the 98 tours and has welcomed guests on it ever since.Dave has said that he lifted the first word from Willie Nelson's Crazy.

songs.title: Cry Freedom
songs.songHistory: This powerful ballad was around since the very beginning where it featured Dave Solo to start out and then the band joining in. It was finally recorded in the studio and released on the "Crash" album; heavily produced and electrified. Its most popular runs were the fall of 1994, the encore features on the Dave and Tim '96 tour. After 1996, the song dropped off into a rarity and was the rarest song on the Summer '97 tour. After being completely missing in action in 1998, the song experienced a small revival in '99, but then dropped off in 2000 and 2001. However, as teases in the Spring 2002 tour showed, the song was surely not dead.  It returned as a Dave solo in the Summer Tour, and full on in 2003.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

songs.title: Cryptorchid
songs.songHistory: This surprising cover of the Marylin Manson original made its appearances on the Dave and Tim '97 tour, and it was often mistakenly called "Crypto-Child" and "Crypto Torture" on tape setlists. Dave ranted a few times about Marylin Manson's music and how he respected his difficult artistic expression. His performances only covered the song's refrain.The title is the adjective form of cryptorchidism, which is the condition of missing one or both testicles.The lyrics that Dave sings are also in Manson's song Antichrist Superstar, which Tim has covered; however, the melody and chord progression are clearly those of Cryptorchid.

songs.title: Dancing in the Moonlight
songs.songHistory: Dave and Tim guested on this song with Michael Franti & Spearhead on 7.2.08

songs.title: Dancing in the Street
songs.songHistory: This song was originally recorded by Martha & the Vandellas in 1964.  It was covered as a duet by Mick Jagger & David Bowie and also famously recorded by Van Halen, The Kinks, Neil Diamond amongst many others.Dave sang a line of it solo during the 7.24.98 show in Camden between songs.

songs.title: Dancing Nancies
songs.songHistory: This simple yet profound number featuring a violin-led climax has been around since the beginning. The now-familiar "Could I have been..." intro developed in early performances, with the first known appearance at 12.9.92, then developing to the 4 current standard lines on 4.2.93 before being arranged into the current order a week later and finally settling into the exact words of their final form on 7.22.93.  An extended spoken intro involving the words "Don't you ever wonder...?" first appeared on 7.20.94.It was recorded for "Under The Table and Dreaming" and was in the top five most played songs consecutively from the summer of 1994 all the way through to 1996. Since then, it has steadly dropped off in number of appearances each tour, only to appear twice in 1999, and once in 2001. Demand by the crowd for the re-establishment of the tune was met during the Summer 2002 tour as it again became a staple of the set.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - acoustic guitarJohn Alagia - vocalsMichael McDonald - vocalsAndrew Page - vocalsJeff Thomas - vocals

songs.title: Death on the High Seas
songs.songHistory: Dave mentioned this song in a video posted in July 2013. It debuted nearly two years later as an intro to Out of My Hands on 5.30.15 (listed on the official dmband.com setlist as "Intro") before making its full debut on 6.12.15.In the aforementioned video, Dave says of the song: "There's a song that I'm working on now, a little piano tune. What's it called? Um... I don't know what I'm calling it. [hums verse] I think it's called 'Death on the High Seas' or something, which is a very happy-sounding title. But it's sort of all about this sort of overwhelming sense of responsibility that's inside of me, that wants to look after my kids. It's an overwhelming feeling, the feeling of what's entrusted to me by biology. And so it gets a little specific, and it reflects political ideas, and it reflects my fear of how the planet, and it reflects my fear of violence towards children and all those things that my children, my kids will face. And that's sort of what it's about. But I don't think I would have written from such a personal perspective before I had kids. I couldn't have, right?"

songs.title: Decline in Reason

songs.title: Deed Is Done
songs.songHistory: It has been said that this song was inspired from a young female fan, "Emily",  who was terminally stricken with leukemia. As the story goes, her last request was to meet Dave Matthews in person, and after visiting her, he was inspired to write "Deed is Done". The song was recorded in Fall of 1995 for the "Crash" album, and was not a "road-tested" tune. It and three other songs recorded ("Help Myself," "Little Thing," and Boyd Tinsley's "True Reflections") were chosen to not be included in the album's line-up. "Deed is Done" was first played during a Dave and Tim live soundcheck for the 9.24.95 show. Has only been played twice by the full band: here and here. The song took its most popular form as a passionate Dave and Tim song where Tim could add his manic sounds to the mood of the song and where Dave has occasionally quoted lines from The Who's "Pinball Wizard". With the release of Before These Crowded Streets in April of 1998, fans heard the "Deed is Done" riff towards the end of the thirty-second jam following "Pig" on the album.

songs.title: Dirty Water
songs.songHistory: "Dirty Water" is a 12-bar blues song composed by Ed Cobb and first recorded by the California rock and roll band The Standells in 1966. The song is a paean to the city of Boston and its then-famously polluted Boston Harbor and Charles River. The Boston Red Sox baseball team is well-known for playing the song immediately following home victories, a tradition that began in the 2001 season. [from Wikipedia]DMB played this song during the encore of the second night at Fenway Park in 2009.

songs.title: Do You Remember
songs.songHistory: Dave wrote this song about the loss of a friend shortly before Farm Aid '17 and debuted it at that festival (9.16.17), having just played it for Tim for the first time earlier in the day.Before the 1.12.18 performance, Dave said that the song is "about things that have gone past that we loved, but that's okay, but things that we loved that are no longer with us."He expanded his explanation of the song at various stops on the 2018 Summer Tour with the most complete one so far in Clarkston on 6.6.18:"So uh, I know that, you know, that we all... there's times in our lives when our hearts get broken, and uh, but that's all part of it, you know because that's part of what makes it so beautiful but uh, but uh, a coup- a little while ago I lost a very good friend of mine and we were having the best day of, uh you know one of those best days where the weather's right, the air is right, and the laughter's right, and everything's right. Anyway, I lost my friend on this beautiful day and I suppose my memory of that day is mostly good except for the end of it. But this song kind of explains a little bit about I feel about my missing of my very good friend."

songs.title: Dodo
songs.songHistory: The following commentary about this song is found in the Some Devil packaging: "Some years ago I was visiting a friend of mine on the coast of Maine. In his house, there was a collection of old National Geographic magazines, some as old as the nineteen-thirties. It was interesting to see how popular American culture viewed Adolf Hitler before the full fury of World War II. The view was quite favorable. It's strange how things change."

songs.title: Don't Burn the Pig
songs.songHistory: This song appeared as a full band number at the famous Yoshi's show in 1995. According to story intros, Dave was supposedly inspired to write the song after watching a nature special in which native tribes burned live pigs to determine judgments. The last time it was played by the full band was on 8.31.96. After that the song disappeared almost entirely with the exception of a few Dave and Tim appearances in 1997.It was not until 1998 during the studio sessions of Before These Crowded Streets where the song was majorly reworked into Pig. Although the phrase, "Don't Burn the Pig" was not included in the album version, it resurfaced in teases of the song in 2001.  When "Pig" returned to the setlists in 2002, it was listed on the road page setlists as "Don't Burn the Pig" but every version has been the modern song.

songs.title: Don't Drink the Water
songs.songHistory: This famous single was produced in the Studio Sessions for "Before These Crowded Streets" from an effort to entirely rework the raw song, "Leave Me Praying." The result was a politically charged song that became the first single from the new album. From the debuting tour of Spring '98 to the Summer of 99, it was the most frequently played song on every tour including Dave and Tim. In the Spring of 2002, it highlighted as one of the top openers for the tour.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - electric guitar/mandolinBéla Fleck - banjoAlanis Morissette - vocals

songs.title: Dream So Real, A
songs.songHistory: This unique song debuted on the Summer 2007 tour. The lyrics to the second regular verse were based fairly substantially on a few Two Step intros from earlier in the tour. After being a somewhat regular feature on the 2007 tour, especially in the opening slot, the song made just one appearance in 2008 and has not been played since.In 2021, Stefan mentioned on Twitter that the band recorded this song at a studio in Seattle while on tour in 2007 or earlier with the intent for it to be used in a film. It was also recorded at Ante Up Studios in Cleveland in August 2007 (it's possible that Stefan just got the studios mixed up and that it was actually only recorded once).

songs.title: Dreamed I Killed God
songs.songHistory: This powerful segue was only featured in the Winter 1997 Dave and Tim tour, usually segueing into Typical Situation. Since the passion in the song was unsurpassed and the profound lyrics were evolving, it enjoyed much scrutiny by fans.

songs.title: Dreaming Tree, The
songs.songHistory: This epic feature from "Before These Crowded Streets" debuted in 1998 where it occasionally featured Greg Howard, Tim Reynolds, and an extended jam masterminded by Carter. Although it enjoyed a mediocre run in the summer of 1999, it disappeared from then until 2003, only to receive the occasional slight tease. After a failed attempt to play it at the last show of the summer 2002 tour the song resurfaced in the summer of 2003 but then disappeared again until summer 2006, when the band extended the jam to almost twice its former length.In 2007, LeRoi interpolated Tay Zonday's "Chocolate Rain" during the jam section of nearly every performance of the song.

songs.title: Dreams of Our Fathers

The bridge is taken directly from the chorus of Little Thing.In a SiriusXM interview before the 5.24.24 broadcast, Stefan said that he brings it up once every couple of years but also admits he doesn't know if they will ever play it.

songs.title: Drive In Drive Out
songs.songHistory: This intense number was there from the very beginning when it was a Dave solo feature for the early days. Much like Tripping Billies was the first dmb song to feature Boyd Tinsley, DIDO was the first song to feature the prowess of Carter Beauford as a full band song. The famous soundcheck of 8.4.92 features the band joyfully shelling it out into a steady feature of 92-93. After only being played once in 1994, the song came in strong in 1995 and peaked in 1996 as one of the most played songs, including one of the top openers AND one of the top closers. Since then, the song enjoyed steady play but has become a rarity since 2005.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

It appeared again on 8.18.07 as a full DMB song.A studio version of the song was released, with an accompanying music video, on iTunes on 9.4.07, with all of the instruments played by Dave.Along with The Long Black Veil, The Maker, Gravedigger, So Damn Lucky, and Stay or Leave, this is one of just six songs that have been played by DMB, Dave & Tim, Dave & Friends, and Dave solo.

songs.title: Everyday
songs.songHistory: This famous song evolved from #36 during the studio sessions for Everyday. Its lyrics partially appeared live as an interpolation in #41 during the Fall 2000 tour. During its first live appearances in the Summer 2001 tour, the song almost immediately drew from its #36 influences; specifically the "Honey, Honey / Come and dance with me" lines. On 5.26.01, the audience shocked the band by chanting the same lines during the show's closer and throughout the encore break. This historic event started a trend of audience participation in the song. Throughout the rest of 2001 and 2002, Dave began resurrecting scatting as well as old lines from #36 developing what is known as the #36 reprise. During Fall of 2002, the audience chants became commonplace as the song was one of the top 5 songs to appear in the encore.

songs.title: Exodus
songs.songHistory: The band has been covering this Bob Marley tune since the very beginning. It has always been a rarity each tour and was never played with any consistency (that we know of, at least; there are MANY missing setlists from 1991-93). After being played only once during 1996, the song disappeared completely only to come back with a shockingly powerful return on 12.13.00 featuring Warren Haynes. The song almost made a return on 8.25.01, but was only teased.  The band's long anticipated return to Red Rocks became a reality in September 2005.  With an additional 4th show announced as a Hurricane Katrina Benefit, this song came back with a montage of guests.

songs.title: Eyes of the World

While the other three songs were played on 3.1.24, this song remains on the shelf.

songs.title: Falling Off the Roof
songs.songHistory: This song, played on the Veillette guitar, was played once in 2007 but then seemed to disappear. In 2009, however, Dave and Carter recorded the song with Herbie Hancock for the latter's album The Imagine Project, released on June 22, 2010. While the album's initial press release listed the song (retitled "Jackpot" from the original "Falling Off the Roof") as appearing on the album and promotional videos showed Dave recording it with Hancock, the song ended up being replaced by a cover of the Beatles' Tomorrow Never Knows. According to an interview with Herbie Hancock, Jackpot was dropped because "Dave decided he hated the song."The song was originally written for an unknown movie's soundtrack, but it was not included in whatever film it was.In early 2017, it was registered with ASCAP as "Falling off the Roof" with "Jackpot" listed as an alternate title.

It is one of several of Phish's staple covers that Trey Anastasio brought to the 2003-04 Dave Matthews & Friends tour, where he sang lead vocals. Instead of the line, "Let Jimi take over," Trey says "Let Timmy take over," referring to Tim Reynolds.The song was also performed in 2011 by all three of Stefan's side-project bands (Yukon Kornelius, Secret Stash, and 1,2,3 GO!).

songs.title: Gas Into Fire
songs.songHistory: This song originated as an interlude with lyrics played prior to 'What Would You Say' on 6.17.04. The riff was also teased several other times later on in the tour. It was originally mislabeled as "Cigarette Lit" before it was identified as a different song altogether. Eventually, it came to be identified as "Gas Into Fire," "Interlude," or "6/17/04 improv" by different sources in the fan community. The song was finally released on the Warehouse 5 vol. 11/Warehouse 8 vol. 8 disc under the title "Gas Into Fire."

songs.title: Gaucho
songs.songHistory: Originally posted on our front page May 19, 2012:"It's not a groundbreaking notion that information spreads faster each year. Yet over the past six months, very little information has been revealed or leaked about DMB's return to the studio. Any sort of official word has come slowly and infrequently. It was rumored that the band was working with Steve Lillywhite (follow @sillywhite)when he was publicly speaking about his time with DMB and it was clear that he was going through old tapes. Then a series of tweets starting in November 2011 (including pictures of Seattle) made it all but official that right after New Year's they would be back in the studio together. Even after this, however, their collaboration still wasn't confirmed until mid-February by management.Near the end of the sessions, Steve started giving a few updates as to where he was in the post-production process and began answering the occasional questions on twitter.  But aside from that, neither he nor management divulged anything about the tracklisting. The first big reveal came on April 24, 2012 when Dave played "Mercy" solo on Late Night with Jimmy Fallon. But the silence was broken with a blast on May 18, 2012 when the band/ management released the studio version of a new, unheard song called "Gaucho" to celebrate the return of the Summer Tour. Contributing to the surprise was the release of a lengthy video tribute to 2010's tour opener the day before. It was worthy of celebrating the opener by itself because included rare archive video of an unreleased show and the full JTR tour opener.A link to the song (click here) was sent out to DMB's mailing list with a unique code for a free download.  However if you don't have a code, they will e-mail you one. The message from Dave was as brief as he usually is: "Hey everybody, here's something new. We had fun making it. Hope you like it, Dave." The mp3 was tagged with generic artwork borrowed from the 2012 Summer Tour logo. A message also narrowed the kno

songs.title: Get in Line
songs.songHistory: This forgotten song made its claim to fame during the 1994 Fall tour as a new song that the band played regularly with alternating lyrics. It was played last on New Year's Eve 1994 and never again made a return despite attempts by fans to remind Dave of the song. It was recorded in the "Crash" sessions, but didn't make the final cut (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998).The song was rumored to have been soundchecked in 1999 to the point where many fans were almost sure of its return. However, it never did and has yet to resurface.

On 12.21.02, James Brown made a surprise appearance in Madison Square Garden to play this song with the band and members from the band "Karl Denison's Tiny Universe".

songs.title: Gotta Travel On
songs.songHistory: "Gotta Travel On" has its origin in British folk music.  It was adapted by Paul Clayton and recorded in 1958 by Billy Grammer, then released as a single a year later and it sold over a million copies and launched Grammer's career.  It was most famously covered by Bob Dylan for his 1970 album Self Portrait.  It has only been played once and it closed a Dave solo show.  Neil Young sat in as a guest for the performance at the 25th Anniversary Bridge Gala in San Francisco on 10.14.11.  Perhaps not coincidentally, Grammer had passed away just two months earlier at the age of 85.As of early 2012, no recording of the performance is in circulation.

songs.title: Grab the Horns by the Bull

songs.songHistory: This country-esque song was written during the "Lillywhite" studio sessions and was one of the later new songs to appear in the 2000 as the #1 most played encore song of the 2000 Summer Tour. Since then it has been played more and more and has evolved an extended jam outro. In the Fall 2002 Tour, it was played every show and featured an intro by Stefan on the slide guitar.

songs.title: Granny
songs.songHistory: This song is one of (if not THE) most popular regularly played songs of all time. If you look at the By Year charts, you'll see that it was played most in 1993 where it was one of the top 3 most played songs through every consecutive tour for that year. All this promotion was probably due to the fact that Granny was supposed to be the band's first 'single'. Matthews admitted that the band envisioned people walking the streets chanting "LOVE! BABY!", and for a while the crowd did chant at several shows during other songs in the setlist. Although it was confirmed to be the only unreleased song recorded for Under The Table and Dreaming (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998), it never quite made it to any other studio albums and is now known for is steady occasional playing. The studio cut was issued on the 2014 20th anniversary release of Under the Table and Dreaming

songs.title: Gravedigger
songs.songHistory: The following commentary about this song is found in the Some Devil packaging: "My father's been dead for more than 20 years. I still want him to be proud of me."In the Spring Tour of 2002, a new intro into PNP > Rapunzel was introducted on 4.26 known as the Gravedigger Intro. This intro developed into the powerful anecdotal song "Gravedigger" in the Summer Tour in 2002, as one of the main feature Dave Solo songs in the encore.Along with The Long Black Veil, Eh Hee, The Maker, So Damn Lucky, and Stay or Leave, this is one of just six songs that have been played by DMB, Dave & Tim, Dave & Friends, and Dave solo.

songs.title: Grey Street
songs.songHistory: This feature song made its debut in 2000 as a frequently played feature with alternating lyrics. In fact, it was the most played song of the 2000 summer tour at 46 of 52 shows.In 2001, this song took a back seat to the Everyday songs yet was still one of the top 5 openers of the tour.After its release on Busted Stuff, the extra chorus at the end omitted, the song came back with a vengeance in 2002 with static lyrics and went on to be the second most played song three tours in a row.  On 12.17.02, the song was played with the first chorus and second verse omitted, likely as a trial run for fitting the song into the time slot for the following night's performance on Late Night with Conan O'Brien. On 12.21.02, the arrangement was switched to 1st verse, 2nd chorus, 2nd verse, 3rd chorus, and it was played that way on all performances for over a year. Starting in the Summer 2004 tour, the song began to be performed in the following form: 1st verse, 1st chorus, 2nd verse, 3rd chorus. This is known as the truncated version. Unless otherwise noted, all performances from 12.17.02 through the end of 2019 are truncated version, while all versions from 2020 on are full versions.While some have speculated that this song is about Dave's ex-girlfriend Julia Grey, there is no evidence for this connection. In fact, it's not even known for sure whether Ms. Grey's last name is spelled that way or as Gray. It seems unlikely that Dave would write a song about an ex-girlfriend seven years after breaking up with her and while engaged to his future wife, and there is nothing in the lyrics that lends any credence to the theory that it is about her aside from the mentions of the color gray (which Dave, having been educated in South Africa, spells the British English way).

songs.title: Hello Again
songs.songHistory: An uncommon "story" song by the band, Hello Again is an old-fashioned first person tale of past murder and the mental anguish that follows.  It debuted as one of the "Summer 2004" songs with Crazy-Easy, Sugar Will and Joyride and was referred to as New Song #3 on the dmband.com websites.  It can be assumed that the song was a late addition to the album because the promotional copies sent to Australia to promote the Spring tour there have it added on to the end of the disc with Joyride after the album closer, Hunger For the Great Light.  Trouble With You was in its place in track 9 after Out of My Hands and before Louisiana Bayou.  When the album was finally released several months later, Trouble With You was removed from the album and added to the bonus disc, while a severely and abruptly truncated version of Hello Again took its place.  The first few notes of Louisiana Bayou were eliminated to make the segue even more disjointed.Live, the song developed throughout the 2004 Summer Tour. Initial chord strummings, which Dave often does before songs, evolved into an intro complete with lyrics, which then evolved (most likely) into the song #27, written before the 2007 Summer Tour.  A rare mistake by Carter mid-tour led to an impromptu drum solo in the middle, which was added into all subsequent versions.  After being a staple of 2004 and early 2005 shows, Hello Again has become a bit of a rarity, and took the entire 2006 Summer Tour off.

songs.title: Help Myself

It was recorded in the "Crash" sessions but didn't make the final cut (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998).Originally they were to have given Halloween for the soundtrack of Scream 2 during the "Before These Crowded Streets" sessions. However, once it was finished, they decided this version of Halloween was too strong, recorded it with the Kronos Quartet and kept it for the album. Then they re-recorded Help Myself and gave it to the movie and soundtrack.

songs.title: I Need a Word
songs.songHistory: This song appeared for the first time on YouTube on September 13, 2013. It was taken from the 44th season premiere of Sesame Street that aired on September 16, 2013. The segment begins with an obviously somber Dave sitting on front porch steps as Grover approaches, sounding as blue as he looks. They commiserate about their melancholy mood until Dave suggests that a song might pick them up. The two then combine on this duet the evokes memories of Dave's most powerful collaborations with the likes of Alanis Morissette and Emmylou Harris. It is also worth noting that Dave plays a 6-string banjo guitar on this song. According to the band's webpage, Dave wrote the song specifically for the show.

songs.title: Idea of You
songs.composer: Beauford / Lessard / Matthews / Moore / Tinsley / Batson
songs.songHistory: Illicit and immoral obsession? Fond remembrance of childhood love? The lyrics taken at face value can make you uncomfortable in many versions. Then again, when we think about ex-loves, we remember them as they were, not as they are now. Some versions seem one way, some versions seem the other, but it's clear the person singing isn't 100% OK with his feelings. In 2007, Dave prefaced the song with "this is a song about my babysitter when I was a kid." The lyrics have changed slightly to fall in line.The Idea of You debuted at the former Deer Creek Music Center on June 2, 2006. It also marked the debut of the Veillette soprano 12-string (originally used exclusively on this and Shotgun), and was the first full band song to be played from the 2006 Spring studio sessions. Of all the 2006 songs, The Idea of You was the most complete when debuted. Its compact structure and standardized harmonic horn duet leave only the lyrics unfinished (though most contain the theme and several lines are consistent).Many times Dave has said afterwards how much he loves the song and it showed with steady play throughout the 2006 Summer Tour.

songs.title: Kill the King
songs.songHistory: This song debuted a few shows into the 2006 tour but fell out of play within a month. After being seemingly forgotten, it made a surprising comeback at the first stop of the 2011 Caravan tour but didn't get played again until the fall 2018 tour.On two occasions (6.9 and 6.24) in 2006, Dave opened the song with a paraphrase of the "I've got some bad news for you, sunshine" line from Pink Floyd's "In the Flesh" (changing "bad" to "awful" both times).

songs.title: Kill the Preacher
songs.songHistory: This song originated in 2012 as an intro to Why I Am with improvised lyrics. Dave again played it before Why I Am on June 1, 2013, but this time he sang from handwritten lyrics, and the official setlist page gave it the title of "Kill the Preacher." After the 7.6.13 show, DMB's official Facebook page began listing it as "Preacher Man" while the dmband.com setlist page continued to list it as "Kill the Preacher." It has yet to appear on any of the printed setlists.Because the 2013 versions have mostly been Dave solo (with some minor accompaniment by Tim and Carter), playing the exact same guitar part as the 2012 versions, we are currently considering all performances to be the same song. However, because the 2012 versions were far more improvised and because the 2013 ones still seem to be works in progress, we are currently looking at the two years separately with regard to whether a performance is a tease, partial, or full. For 2012, we have labeled the relatively lengthy, full-band versions as "full" while calling the medium-length ones "partials" and the shortest ones "teases." For 2013 and on, a full version is one with two refrains, a verse, and a bridge.

songs.title: Lie in Our Graves
songs.songHistory: Lie In Our Graves is one of the band's the oldest songs, first appearing in early 1992.  It features Dave’s “carpe diem” message, and as Dave has mentioned several times, encourages listeners to have no regrets after dying.  By the end of 1992, a slight Carter solo developed and through the first few shows of 1993, the format which eventually became the staple for the song was established, with LeRoi taking a solo between the main body and the reprise.  Although the extended bridge originally featured LeRoi, after the Crash sessions Boyd took over the solo spot on 4.30.1996 (Crash release show).  The last show to feature a LeRoi solo was 12.5.1995.  After being included on the Crash album, this fan favorite was played at each show on the Fall and New Year’s Eve Run Tours.  It is also currently in the top ten in all time plays after being in heavy rotation all the way up to 2001 and still frequents a fair of amount sets since then.Lie In Our Graves’s structure also allows for a variety of guests.  The elongated bridge section has featured artists such as Big Voice Jack (6.5.1998), David Hidalgo (7.6.1997), Trey Anastasio (5.19.2001) and Butch Taylor on keys.  Preceding the reprise on several versions in 2000 (starting on the West Coast run and including 8.29.2000 – Live Trax 11), Dave takes a moment to refer to Butch as “number 6.”  After a handful of appearances on it in 1996, Béla Fleck emerged as possibly the premiere guest soloist on the song during the 1997 Tour, and has been featured many times since.  Of special note, on 11.21.1998 Boyd and Béla both took solos for the first time.  On the Crash album, Tim Reynolds was featured in the solo spot, and a game of table tennis can be heard in the background. Over the years, Lie In Our Graves has seen many changes.  Its first known version is 3.1.92 On the first known recording, Dave ends the song singing, “’till we go away.”  This was sung at the end of many versions until after 3.24.1993 when “till we dance away” w

songs.title: Litho Blitho
songs.albumName: Sounds of Wood and Steel, Vol. 3
songs.songHistory: This instrumental number was supposedly recorded by Dave during the studio sessions for his "Some Devil" album. It was officially released on a compilation CD called Sounds of Wood and Steel, Vol. 3. The riff is exactly that of the Mother Father bridge/outro, and the words "litho" and "blitho" supposedly mean mother and father in an African language (note: we suspect that this is false, much like the apocryphal translation of "Pantala Naga Pampa" meaning "welcome to our home" in Gambian, which is not a language). Also worth noting about the title is that it was recorded in Studio Litho, which is Stone Gossard's studio in Seattle ("litho" is a Greek root word for "stone").

songs.title: Little Red Bird
songs.songHistory: Little Red Bird is a song released on the Big Whiskey and the Groogrux King bonus disc, also titled Little Red Bird.  It made its live debut at a Dave & Tim show on 12.10.09 in Las Vegas, the first of three nights.  It was also played 12.12.09 (the third night), marking its live release debut on the Live in Las Vegas full show release.  The song made its debut at a full band show in the encore, though played solo by Dave, on 6.25.10 in Cuyahoga Falls.  At that show, Dave gave the following introduction providing some background into the writing of the song:"I was visiting, a few years ago with a friend of mine, I was visiting these little tiny islands in the middle of the Indian Ocean and there was this little, little bird and it had just one leg.  But it would come and bounce around underneath the chair, and so you knew it was that bird 'cause I'm sure there's just not a bunch of tiny little red birds with one leg. So then I wrote this song over there a couple years ago or something like that."

songs.title: Little Thing
songs.songHistory: During the 4.30.96 performance of this song, Dave refers to the common story spoken over the intro as occurring in January of 1996.This was released as An' Another Thing on Dave's solo release Some Devil with a string background. Current versions are still labeled as "Little Thing," and this isn't moved to defunct because a.) when played on 1.14.04, the official road page labeled it as Little Thing, b.) there was no structural change with the name change, c.) a post-Some Devil release, Live Trax volume 4 (4.30.96) still had it labeled as "Little Thing," and d.) all post-Some Devil stage setlists for which we have scans list it as "Little Thing."In an interview published in the Wall Street Journal on March 6, 2013, Dave was asked which song of his he secretly loves, and he responded, "Little Thing, which is the only song I have recorded that has no words."The chorus was borrowed for the bridge in Dreams of Our Fathers.Because the structure of this song is so fluid and has no set lyrics, it's difficult to categorize performances into "tease" vs. "partial" vs. "full." For our site's purposes, we call any performance with four or more significant sections (verses or choruses) a "full version," and any performance with fewer than two significant parts a "tease" ("significant" here is still fairly subjective, but it means at least 15 seconds' worth of that part's guitar riff).

While Dave's lyrics are almost completely improvised, the song is obviously based on "If I Had Words."It's not really accurate to call this a cover, but it's also not really an original, and we have to put it in one category or the other.

Additionally, it was first acknowledged on the printed setlists on 12/14/2002.Loving Wings has been released 6 times on a live album. Of all songs to not appear on a studio album, it has been released the most. It is one of the few unreleased songs to have been played at least once a year since its inception. songs.title: Memory Motel
songs.songHistory: Dave sings backup on this song on the Rolling Stones' 1998 album No Security. Dave performed this song with the Stones five times on their 1998 tour of Europe, and the version on this record is taken from the July 5 performance.Dave has recounted that he was in the bathtub in Amsterdam when he got a call from Mick Jagger about guesting with them on this song, which he had never heard before. He pretended that he knew the song and then had to find a copy of the Black and Blue album to learn it. On the first night, the Teleprompter didn't advance, so it just said "Memory Motel" for the duration of the song.

songs.title: Mercy
songs.songHistory: With only one day's notice, Dave debuted Mercy on the 4.24.12 episode of Late Night with Jimmy Fallon, with the Roots' "Captain" Kirk Douglas accompanying him on acoustic guitar and backing vocals (the Roots are Jimmy Fallon's house band).  Stefan confirmed on twitter that it was a full band song.  Dave played it solo at few benefits in the next two weeks before the Summer Tour started.The full version debuted at the Woodlands tour opener 5.18.12 as part of a three song run of the new album songs.  That performance revealed that Rashawn would handle the harmonies and after starting on the guitar, Dave moved to the piano for an instrumental outro that was not featured on the earlier versions.  Starting at Scranton on 5.28.12, the outro melted in Out of My Hands (with Dave already at the piano).  This combination of songs would continue through the tour with about half of the subsequent versions continuing with the segue.  Like the other songs from the new album, Mercy was played consistently during the tour, but not every night.On June 27th, with the album announcement, management also revealed that Mercy would be the lead single of their upcoming album "Away From the World." A youtube video of the studio version was released that was fairly identical to the live versions, with the addition of strings in the outro. Steve Lillywhite on his twitter account said the radio edit would end at the end of Dave's lyrics and not contain the outro.

songs.title: Minarets
songs.songHistory: The studio version on Remember Two Things was recorded at Flat Five Studios in Salem, VA.  This song famously features a live "Scat" intro where Dave scats along to his muted guitar strumming before starting the main riff of the song.On versions from late 1993 to 1995, Carter would also scat along with Dave in the intro. Below are notes about the "Slow and Low" intro:During 1994, Carter interpolated lyrics from the Run-DMC song "Slow and Low" (better known as recorded by the Beastie Boys) during the scat. Here are the lyrics Carter covers:"I do not sing, but I make a songYou'll live your whole life, and I hope you'll live longOn the Gong Show we won't get gongedIt's Run-DMC, not Cheech and Chong"He says it very scattishly, and sometimes it's hard to make out. He also says, "It's (fuckin') DMB", instead of "It's Run-DMC". After this he continues the scat with Dave, often saying something like, "I'm talkin' about Dave...I'm talkin' about Dave", until the main riff hits.It is known that Carter started doing it sometime in early '94 (verified on 1.3.94) and stopped doing it sometime in late '94 (verified on 11.14.94) until he started it again in 2014.Studio personnel:Greg Howard - Chapman stick/synthesizer/percussion samplesTim Reynolds - guitar

songs.title: Nature
songs.songHistory: The song "Nature" is a Samples cover, released for the first time in May of 1989 as the last track on their self-titled album.  Also known as "The Blue Album," the record was recorded in 1989 and released that year independently by the band. The album caught the attention of executives at Arista records, and the Samples signed their first record contract. The Blue Album was re-released on the Arista label, but the Samples left the label before recording another album, citing irreconcilable differences between the band and the label. The album was released for a third time in 1991 when the band signed with fledgling What Are Records?Dave used to sing some lyrics from "Nature" before Tripping Billies overtop of some chords based around Tripping Billies. For this reason, this intro is known as the "Nature intro" even though it is instrumental now and therefore has no connection to the Samples song.When the intro is listed as a separate song, it refers to an extended Nature intro whose music is still based around Tripping Billies but which has lyrics from the Samples song.

songs.title: Needle and the Damage Done, The

It is about the drug addiction of the late Crazy Horse guitarist Danny Whitten and was released on Young's 1972 Harvest. The version released on the album is a live recording from January 30, 1971 at Royce Hall on the campus of UCLA.Subsequently it was played sporadically at Dave solo, Dave & Tim and full band shows throughout 2010, though always Dave solo with the exception of 6.5.10 where Carter joined with some light shaker accompaniment.  Most versions took place in the encore of shows.  It was not played at all in 2011.The cover is represented in DMB's live catalog on the boxed set exclusive 9.17.10 Live at Wrigley Field release.

songs.title: Oh
songs.songHistory: First verse teased by Dave on 8.26.02.  Chorus teased on the 2003 Dave and Tim tour, both by itself on 3.25.03 and during the Everyday outro on 3.28.03.  It was played in full by Dave and Friends at almost every show of the 2003-2004 Winter Tour and has been a staple of both Dave & Tim and Dave solo shows ever since.Dave (9.23.12): "You know, a lot of people, you fall in and out of love and you fall in love and you find someone and all that... I tell you what, my grandfather and my grandmother (my mom's parents), my mom said when she was little they were so in love that she almost sometimes felt that they didn't even know that they had a couple kids. 'Cause they were crazy in love. And that's sometimes, you know. It doesn't happen to all of us, but you know. But you could almost touch how totally in love my grandparents were. And then even after my grandmother died, it was like this funny echo in the room. And my grandfather, he didn't dawdle, he didn't think about romantic things that often. But boy was he in love. Anyway they were in love and kind of wrote this song about them."
